learn.title: Hydrogen-Powered Green Steel Production
learn.intro: Green steel replaces coal with hydrogen in blast furnaces, cutting CO2 emissions by up to 90%. This innovation is critical for decarbonizing heavy industry, a major contributor to global emissions.
learn.c1_text: In traditional steelmaking, coal reacts with iron ore at 2,000°C, releasing 2 tons of CO2 per ton of steel. Hydrogen acts as a cleaner 'reducing agent,' producing water vapor instead. Pilot plants in Sweden already demonstrate this shift.
learn.c2_text: Producing green hydrogen requires renewable electricity via electrolysis. At scale, this demands 50 GW of clean power by 2030 for 10% global steel demand. Infrastructure costs remain a barrier, but subsidies in the EU and US are accelerating adoption.
learn.c3_text: India’s steel sector contributes 8% of national emissions. The Ministry of Steel aims for 10% green steel by 2030, partnering with firms like Tata Steel to test hydrogen-based DRI (Direct Reduced Iron) plants.
learn.stat2: Global green steel market to reach $90 billion by 2030 (IEA)
learn.stat3: Hydrogen production cost: $3–5/kg (vs. $1.5/kg for grey hydrogen)
learn.stat4: EU’s HYBRISC project targets 10 MT green steel/year by 2025

learn.title: Wind Resource Assessment Techniques
learn.intro: Wind resource assessment identifies optimal turbine locations using historical data and modeling. This ensures efficient energy capture, as wind speed directly impacts power output (power ∝ wind speed³).
learn.c1_text: Anemometers and LiDAR measure wind speed/direction at heights above 100m. A 1 m/s increase in wind speed boosts energy output by 3.3%. Developers use 10+ years of data for accuracy.
learn.c2_text: Tools like WAsP simulate wind flow using terrain maps. In India, sites like Kanyakumari (wind speed: 8 m/s average) are prioritized. Turbine placement avoids ‘wakes’ to minimize energy loss.
learn.c3_text: India’s wind capacity reached 42 GW in 2023, with Gujarat leading. The National Institute of Wind Energy (NIWE) classifies regions into ‘Excellent,’ ‘Good,’ and ‘Fair’ categories, guiding ₹1.5 lakh crore investments.

learn.title: The Duck Curve Challenge
learn.intro: Solar’s midday peak creates a ‘duck curve’ in grid demand charts, requiring rapid ramping of fossil fuels at sunset. This strains grids and increases emissions if not managed.
learn.c1_text: In California, solar meets 50% of midday demand but drops to 10% by evening. Fossil plants must ramp up 40% in 2 hours, risking inefficiency and wear.
learn.c2_text: Batteries and demand response programs (e.g., time-of-use pricing) flatten the curve. Germany uses 10 GW of storage to manage its duck curve, avoiding €1.2 billion in peak costs.
learn.c3_text: India’s grid faces similar challenges with 70 GW of solar. The 2022 National Electricity Plan mandates 15 GW of battery storage by 2027. States like Tamil Nadu use smart grids to balance solar+storage.
learn.stat2: Duck curve reduction: 30% with batteries (US DOE)

learn.title: Floating Solar Farms on Water
learn.intro: Floating solar installations on reservoirs reduce water evaporation by 70% and provide stable power generation. Ideal for water-scarce regions.
learn.c1_text: In Japan, a 500 MW floating farm saves 2.4 million tons of water annually. The shade from panels reduces reservoir evaporation, preserving drinking water.
learn.c2_text: Cooler water temperatures improve panel efficiency (+0.5% per °C drop). Floating systems avoid land-use conflicts, with 2.8 MW/km² capacity vs. 1.5 MW/km² on land.
learn.c3_text: India’s first floating solar plant in Ramagundam (Andhra Pradesh) generates 600 MW. The 2022 National Solar Mission targets 10 GW floating solar by 2030, leveraging 5 million hectares of reservoirs.
learn.stat4: India’s target: 10 GW floating solar by 2030

learn.title: Electricity Grid Balancing
learn.intro: Grids balance supply/demand in real time using forecasting, storage, and flexible generation. Imbalances risk blackouts; renewable variability intensifies this challenge.
learn.c1_text: Grid operators use 5-minute forecasts to dispatch resources. A 1% forecast error can cause 10 MW of imbalance. Inertia from coal plants is being replaced by synthetic solutions.
learn.c2_text: Transmission upgrades and smart grids enable power sharing across regions. Denmark imports wind power when local supply is low, maintaining 99.9% grid stability.
learn.c3_text: India’s grid loses ₹1.1 lakh crore/year to imbalances. The 2022 Grid Code mandates 5-minute frequency control. States like Rajasthan use AI to predict solar/wind output.

learn.title: Battery Management Systems (BMS)
learn.intro: BMS monitors and controls battery cells to prevent overheating, overcharging, and degradation. This ensures safety, longevity, and efficiency for EVs and grid storage.
learn.c1_text: A BMS balances cell voltages to avoid thermal runaway. For example, Tesla’s BMS limits charging to 80% for daily use, extending battery life to 1,500+ cycles.
learn.c2_text: Algorithms predict degradation and adjust charging. Lithium-ion batteries lose 2–3% capacity/year without BMS. Fast-charging EVs use BMS to maintain 90% efficiency at high currents.
learn.c3_text: India’s EV market needs 500 GW of battery storage by 2030. AMSVITECH’s BMS reduces thermal runaway risks, critical for tropical climates. FAME policy mandates BMS in all EVs.
learn.stat1: India’s EV battery demand: 500 GWh by 2030
learn.stat2: BMS cost: 10–15% of battery pack price
